answer: According to the ghazal 'Aah ko chahiye ik umr asar hote tak' (Diwan-E-Ghalib, chapter 3), a sigh must wait an entire age (umr — a lifetime) before it can take effect. The opening couplet states: 'A sigh must wait an age to take its flight / Who lives to conquer your dark hair's long night?' The poem's central conceit is that the processes of love and longing operate on immense, almost impossible timescales — a sigh's journey to effect is so protracted that no one lives long enough to see it fulfilled. This is reinforced later in the ghazal where the speaker laments that he will turn to dust before even a message of care reaches him. The sigh, then, is an emblem of deferred, perhaps never-to-be-realised, effect: it requires a whole lifetime merely to begin to work.
